name: Improper Management of Sensitive Trace Data
description: Trace data collected from several sources on the System-on-Chip (SoC) is stored in unprotected locations or transported to untrusted agents.
extended_description: To facilitate verification of complex System-on-Chip (SoC) designs, SoC integrators add specific IP blocks that trace the SoC's internal signals in real-time. This infrastructure enables observability of the SoC's internal behavior, validation of its functional design, and detection of hardware and software bugs. Such tracing IP blocks collect traces from several sources on the SoC including the CPU, crypto coprocessors, and on-chip fabrics. Traces collected from these sources are then aggregated inside trace IP block and forwarded to trace sinks, such as debug-trace ports that facilitate debugging by external hardware and software debuggers. Since these traces are collected from several security-sensitive sources, they must be protected against untrusted debuggers. If they are stored in unprotected memory, an untrusted software debugger can access these traces and extract secret information. Additionally, if security-sensitive traces are not tagged as secure, an untrusted hardware debugger might access them to extract confidential information.

mapping.rationale: This CWE entry is at the Base level of abstraction, which is a preferred level of abstraction for mapping to the root causes of vulnerabilities.

mechanism.root_cause_summary: Trace data collected from several sources on the System-on-Chip (SoC) is stored in unprotected locations or transported to untrusted agents.
typical_preconditions: untrusted or insufficiently validated state reaches sensitive operation
typical_sources: untrusted or insufficiently validated state reaches sensitive operation
negative_nonfix_signals: style refactor without changing the sensitive path

guard_patterns: same logical object and same sink context

recommended_guard_templates: same logical object and same sink context

stage1.goal: Classify patch chunks by how directly they repair the CWE root cause.
must_focus: direct change to vulnerable sink or validation site
changes that alter boundary, lifecycle, policy, or authorization relation
supporting helper or error-propagation logic needed by the main fix
must_avoid: generic logging changes without security semantics
refactors unrelated to the sensitive sink/path

stage2.goal: Construct discriminative root cause, anchor, predicates, and guards.
must_focus: anchor at the sink or dominant validation block
prefer predicates that encode relations, not lone generic tokens
choose function-local or anchor-window scope whenever possible
must_avoid: predicates that rely on generic names alone
anchors in wrappers that lack the core vulnerability mechanism
recommended_queries: same logical object and same sink context

stage3.goal: Verify whether the target tag still exhibits the vulnerable relation or already contains the fix relation.
must_focus: whether the sensitive sink/path still exists
whether the fix relation is locally present in the same code region
whether anchor relocation is required before concluding absence
must_avoid: declaring NOT_AFFECTED from generic token absence alone
using broad token matches as final evidence

bad_examples.summary: In a SoC, traces generated from sources include security-sensitive IP blocks such as CPU (with tracing information such as instructions executed and memory...
bad_examples.snippet: The traces do not have any privilege level attached to them. All collected traces can be viewed by any debugger (i.e., SoC designer, OEM debugger, or end user).

good_examples.summary: In a SoC, traces generated from sources include security-sensitive IP blocks such as CPU (with tracing information such as instructions executed and memory...
good_examples.snippet: Some of the traces are SoC-design-house secrets, while some are OEM secrets. Few are end-user secrets and the rest are not security-sensitive. Tag all traces with the appropriate, privilege level at the source. The bits...
